result:
Morning arrives like a rumor, soft at first, then everywhere. The kettle clicks, the window brightens, and a bird argues with the invisible administration of the sky. I sit at the table with both hands around a chipped mug, waiting for the world to become specific. There is mail to answer, laundry to fold, messages blinking like small lighthouses on distant, managerial coasts. Yet for a minute I refuse the summons. Steam rises. Dust turns gold in the beam by the bookshelf. The day has not chosen its shape. It is still possible to become anyone at all again today.

I remember a creek behind my grandmother's house, though perhaps I have improved it with remembering. In memory the water is clearer, the stones more deliberate, the dragonflies lacquered blue by a patient artist. My cousins and I built dams from mud and leaves, then destroyed them with the solemnity of weather. We believed every frog was a secret prince, every rusted bottle cap a coin from an empire swallowed by weeds. Adults called us in for sandwiches. We arrived barefoot, criminally happy, carrying the smell of sun warmed grass, and the evidence of our kingdom drying on our knees.

Cities teach a different alphabet. Sirens are punctuation; crosswalks are brief negotiations with fate. At night, apartment windows float above the street like pages from a thousand unfinished novels, each lit rectangle containing someone reheating soup, practicing guitar, crying quietly, flossing, laughing into a phone. I like the generosity of not knowing. The stranger beside me on the train has a life as dense and weathered as mine, full of passwords, promises, old grudges, favorite socks, private superstitions. We lean together when the rails bend, a temporary congregation swaying under advertisements for dentists, language courses, and impossible vacations in paradise.

My pocket contains a machine that can show me storms on other continents, the face of a friend aging in real time, recipes, wars, jokes, maps, hymns, markets, and the exact distance to the moon. It can also make me feel lonely in a room full of people. I keep touching it, as if it were a wound or a small oracle. Sometimes it rewards me with a blue spark of belonging. Sometimes it pours gravel into my attention. I am learning to set it down and listen for the older notifications: hunger, fatigue, birdsong, conscience, wind, and actual weather.

Writing begins with embarrassment. A blank page is too honest; it sees the theatrical way you clear your throat. You place one sentence down, then another, like planks across a swamp. Some sink. Some hold. Soon there is a path, crooked but usable, and beyond it a clearing you did not plan. I distrust the myth of inspiration as lightning. More often it is a moth, foolishly persistent, bumping the lampshade until you notice it. The trick is to leave a light on, forgive the wings, and follow whatever small shadow trembles against the wall tonight and calls you onward.

Once, at a hospital, I watched an old man feed his wife ice chips from a paper cup. He held each sliver to her mouth with the concentration of a jeweler setting a stone. Nothing about the room was romantic: curtains, wires, soap smell, television murmuring beyond the door. Yet love was there, immense and unadvertised, surviving without flowers or violin music. It had become a hand that did not shake, a voice saying, here, one more, easy now. I think of them whenever I am tempted to measure devotion by declarations instead of by attendance quietly kept over years.

Outside my window, a plane tree practices patience. It has accepted seasons I would have argued with: the blunt shears of winter, the green riots of April, the dusty exhaustion of August, the ceremonial fire of October. Its roots drink from darkness. Its branches write messy cursive against the sky. Children have kicked balls into it; dogs have blessed it; lovers have carved initials in its bark and then become other people. Still it stands, neither saint nor statue, merely alive in the complete verb of being. I hope to grow less clever and more like a tree someday, maybe.

There is a peculiar holiness in ordinary kitchens after midnight. The refrigerator hums like a sleepy monk. A single spoon waits in the sink, silver and reproachful. Bread rests inside its bag, dreaming of toast. The house has loosened its collar; even the floorboards speak softly. I have stood in that bluish quiet eating cereal from a bowl and felt, absurdly, forgiven. Not for any grand crime, but for the daily inventory of failures: impatience, envy, cowardice, wasted afternoons. Milk, sugar, silence, and darkness formed a temporary chapel. Morning would come with errands; mercy had already arrived without making noise.

The future is not a tunnel, though we often speak of it that way, as if we are trapped on rails toward a known mouth of light. It is more like weather gathering over a sea we are still naming. Some clouds are ours, manufactured by appetite and fear; others drift in from distances beyond blame. We build boats anyway. We teach children to knot rope, to read stars, to share oranges, to apologize. Hope is not certainty wearing a bright coat. Hope is the stubborn maintenance of oars, even while the horizon mutters and gulls vanish into gray distance.

If I had to offer a prayer, it would be small enough to fit inside a matchbox. Let me notice. Let me be useful. Let me laugh before the joke is gone. Let me put down the stone I have been carrying for no reason I can remember. Let me love the flawed world without lying about its wounds. Let me rest when rest is wiser than conquest. Let me begin again when beginning again is the only honest art. The kettle cools, the bird returns, and the unfinished day leans toward me, open handed, still waiting for an answer.
